findings.title: Suspending a borrow market makes solvent positions immediately liquidatable

best_agent_match.title: Suspending a borrow market makes solvent positions immediately liquidatable

rejected_candidates.title: Oracle price manipulation allows stealing all protocol funds
rejected_candidates.reason: Admin-only oracle replacement is an explicit privileged governance power, not a permissionless code flaw or privilege escalation.

rejected_candidates.title: Unrestricted interest rate model enables rate manipulation
rejected_candidates.reason: Setting the interest model is an explicit admin control; this is governance trust/centralization, not a standalone vulnerability in the implementation.

rejected_candidates.title: No maximum cap on origination fee allows 100%+ fees
rejected_candidates.reason: This is an admin misconfiguration/griefing risk rather than a realistic permissionless exploit or invariant break.

rejected_candidates.title: All supported markets automatically become collateral
rejected_candidates.reason: Market support and collateral admission are both admin-controlled configuration choices; without a separate privilege bypass, this is governance design rather than a code bug.

rejected_candidates.title: Suspended markets still count for collateral calculations
rejected_candidates.reason: This behavior is explicitly documented in `_suspendMarket`; the report does not show an independent exploit beyond the kept finding that suspended borrow markets become liquidatable.

rejected_candidates.title: No timelock on admin actions allows instant privileged changes
rejected_candidates.reason: Lack of a timelock is a governance-centralization concern, not a contract vulnerability by itself.

rejected_candidates.title: Floating pragma version may cause compatibility issues
rejected_candidates.reason: Best-practice concern only; no concrete exploit path or protocol-level harm is shown.

rejected_candidates.title: Self-destruct possible through admin-controlled equity withdrawal
rejected_candidates.reason: `_withdrawEquity` is an explicit admin function limited to protocol equity and does not constitute an unintended exploit path.
